content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Consolidation with mitigation (closure as default, compensatory measures)'. Hungary's statutory framework grants maintainers broad discretion to close or reorganize rural primary schools, but multiple procedural safeguards (annual implementation deadlines, mandatory consultation, phase-out options, maintainer-change alternatives, and political-legal caution) collectively slow the speed and frequency of closures below what legal authority alone would permit.
opportunities: Enables investment concentration in fewer, better-equipped consolidated sites, following the Portuguese centros escolares pattern of matched receiving-site investment.
Statutory transport guarantees and targeted support provide a formal, legally enforceable floor of protection for displaced pupils, an improvement over ad hoc arrangements.
strengths: The National Public Education Act (Nkt. 2011. évi CXC. törvény) establishes that primary schools can be maintained by the state, municipalities, nationality self-governments, church legal entities, or authorized private organizations, creating a formal plurality of maintainers (fenntartók) with distinct legal identities and decision authority.

School reorganization or closure decisions by state-maintained institutions (tankerület) must be decided no later than the last working day of May for implementation in the following school year, creating a hard annual procedural deadline that structurally limits the speed of closure implementation.
threats: Compensatory measures (transport guarantees, TEIP-style support) require sustained multi-year funding; without a specified binding funding-commitment mechanism (e.g., ring-fenced budget line, multi-year statutory allocation), these measures risk being cut in future budget cycles once the initial closure has already occurred and political attention has moved on.

Closure decisions, even with consultation, may trigger community mobilization (as in the 2007 Erdőkürt 'Human Chain for Rural Schools' precedent), and limited judicial review (legality-only, not policy-merit) means affected communities have few formal channels to contest closure substance.
weaknesses: No primary legislative source or government regulatory document was located that establishes an explicit statutory minimum enrollment threshold (e.g., 'a school with fewer than N students must close' or 'must be reorganized'), as exists in some comparative jurisdictions; it is unclear whether such thresholds operate as informal administrative practice or guidance without statutory force.

The scope and current enforceability of the pre-2010 requirement for independent expert assessment before school closure is not confirmed in force or codified in the current Nkt. or subordinate regulations; it is unclear whether this procedural gate remains mandatory, optional, or obsolete.
